---
title: "3.8 Scope Levels and Local Variables"
subtitle: "Scope and Access"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Scope and Access

### Topic intro

The **scope** of a variable is defined as where a variable is accessible or can be used.

The scope is determined by where you declare the variable when you write your programs.

When you declare a variable, look for the closest enclosing curly braces (`{}`); this is its scope.

## Class, Method, and Block Level Scope

### Three levels

Java has three levels of scope that correspond to different types of variables:

::: {.tight-list}
- **Class Level Scope** for **instance variables** inside a class.
- **Method Level Scope** for **local variables**, including **parameter variables**, inside a method.
- **Block Level Scope** for **loop variables** and other local variables defined inside blocks of code with `{}`.
:::

## Scope Diagram {.image-fit}

### Class, method, and block level scope

![Class, Method, and Block Level Scope](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-8-scope-and-access/scopeDiagram.png){fig-align="center" width="62%"}

The figure shows the three levels of scope.

## Check Your Understanding

### `clickablearea:: name_class_scope`

Click on all the variable declarations that are at Class Level Scope.

```java
public class Name
{
    private String first;
    public String last;

    public Name(String theFirst, String theLast)
    {
        String firstName = theFirst;
        first = firstName;
        last = theLast;
    }
}
```

## Answer Reasoning

### Class-level declarations

The class-level declarations are:

```java
private String first;
public String last;
```

They are instance variables declared at the top of the class, inside the class body, but outside the constructor.

## Check Your Understanding

### `clickablearea:: name_method_scope`

Click on all the variable declarations that are at Method Level Scope.

```java
public class Name
{
    private String first;
    public String last;

    public Name(String theFirst, String theLast)
    {
        String firstName = theFirst;
        first = firstName;
        last = theLast;
    }
}
```

## Answer Reasoning

### Method-level declarations

The method-level declarations are:

```java
String theFirst
String theLast
String firstName = theFirst;
```

The two parameters and the local variable declared inside the constructor have method-level scope.

## Local Variables

### Declared in headers or bodies

**Local variables** are variables declared in the headers or bodies of blocks of code.

Local variables can only be accessed in the block in which they are declared.

Local variables that are declared inside a method are usually declared at the top of the method.

These variables can only be used within the method and do not exist outside of the method.

## Parameters Are Local

### Constructor and method parameters

Parameter variables are also considered local variables that only exist for that method or constructor.

These variables may only be used within the constructor or method and cannot be declared to be `public` or `private`.

It is good practice to declare any variables that are used by just one method as local variables in that method.

## Instance Variables and Scope

### Class scope

Instance variables at class scope are shared by all the methods in the class.

They can be marked as `public` or `private` with respect to their access outside of the class.

They have class scope regardless of whether they are `public` or `private`.

Another way to look at scope is that a variable's scope is where it lives and exists.

You cannot use the variable in code outside of its scope.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: PersonScope`

Try the following code to see that you cannot access the variables outside of their scope levels in the `toString()` method.

Explain to someone sitting next to you why you cannot access these.

Try to fix the errors by either using variables that are in scope or moving the variable declarations so that the variables have larger scope.

## PersonScope Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Person
{
    private String name;
    private String email;

    public Person(String initName, String initEmail)
    {
        name = initName;
        email = initEmail;
    }

    public String toString()
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)
        {
            int id = i;
        }
        // Can you access the blockScope variables i or id?
        // TODO: Fix the scope error for i
        System.out.println("i at the end of the loop is " + i);
        // TODO: Fix the scope error for id
        System.out.println("The last id is " + id);

        // Can toString() access parameter variables in Person()?
        // TODO: Return variables that are in scope here
        return initName + ": " + initEmail;
    }

    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // call the constructor to create a new person
        Person p1 = new Person("Sana", "sana@gmail.com");
        System.out.println(p1);
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The tests check for:

::: {.tight-list}
- returning the instance variables with `return name + ": " + email;`
- declaration and initialization of `id` to `0` with `int id = 0;`
:::

## Same Name Local Variable

### Local shadows instance variable

If there is a local variable with the same name as an instance variable, the variable name will refer to the local variable instead of the instance variable.

In the next lesson, we can distinguish between the local variable and the instance variable using the keyword `this` to refer to this object's instance variables.

## PersonLocalVar Code

### `activecode:: PersonLocalVar`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Person
{
    private String name;
    private String email;

    public Person(String initName, String initEmail)
    {
        name = initName;
        email = initEmail;
    }

    public String toString()
    {
        String name = "unknown";
        // The local variable name here will be used,
        // not the instance variable name.
        return name + ": " + email;
    }

    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // call the constructor to create a new person
        Person p1 = new Person("Sana", "sana@gmail.com");
        System.out.println(p1);
    }
}
```
:::

## Trace Check

### Expected output

The source asks: What will the code print out?

The expected output from `main` is:

```text
unknown: sana@gmail.com
```

The local variable `name` in `toString()` is used instead of the instance variable `name`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define scope as where a variable is accessible or can be used
- identify class-level, method-level, and block-level declarations
- explain why local variables exist only inside their block or method
- explain why constructor parameters are local variables
- fix `PersonScope` by using variables that are in scope
- trace why `PersonLocalVar` prints `unknown`
:::

## End

### 3.8 Part 1 complete

Next: debugging scope violations.
